# Flavorweaver Recipe Identity Standard

Status: Active canon  
Applies to: Don & Amy World-Culture Collection, public HTML recipe pages, Markdown source files  
Established: 2026-06-30

---

## Core Principle

Flavorweaver recipes are the working recipes of the Amy & Don Kitchen.

The public site is primarily for Amy, Don, family, and trusted household cooking reference. Public recipe pages do not need defensive language, authenticity disclaimers, or labels that explain why a recipe was adapted.

The recipe itself should be confident, clean, cookable, and useful.

---

## Public HTML Standard

Public HTML pages should answer one question:

> How do we make this recipe well?

Public pages should include:

- clean recipe title
- summary
- culture/category/status metadata when useful
- measured ingredient roll-up
- measured ingredients by component
- stage-based prep bowls with measurements
- do-not-forget reminders
- full cooking timeline and instructions
- taste target
- pairings or required components when useful
- print-friendly structure

Public HTML pages should not add extra explanation unless Amy explicitly asks for it.

Avoid public-facing labels such as:

- Amy & Don House Version
- pantry bridge version
- not authentic
- adapted because...
- substitute version
- budget version
- local-ingredient version

Those ideas may be true and useful internally, but they do not need to appear on the public recipe page by default.

The recipe title and ingredient list are enough.

---

## Markdown Source Standard

Markdown source files are the source-of-truth kitchen record.

They may preserve deeper development context, including:

- research notes
- traditional backbone notes
- substitution logic
- ingredient-access limitations
- pantry notes
- future upgrade paths
- Amy and Don test verdicts
- rating notes
- change logs
- what worked
- what failed
- what should be tested next

This information belongs in Markdown because it helps future recipe development without cluttering the cook-facing public page.

---

## Adaptation Standard

Flavorweaver does not measure success by authenticity alone.

For world-culture recipes, the development process should still begin with respect and research:

1. Understand the real dish or technique.
2. Compare strong source patterns when possible.
3. Identify what gives the dish its flavor architecture.
4. Adapt intelligently for the Amy & Don Kitchen.
5. Use ingredients Amy can realistically obtain locally, affordably, or through available grocery systems.
6. Test against the real standard: would Amy and Don want to make it again?

The final recipe may be inspired by a culture, restaurant meal, family memory, printed recipe, pantry constraint, or Flavorweaver test. Once developed, it is a Flavorweaver recipe.

---

## Naming Standard

Recipe names should be clean, confident, and practical.

Use honest style words only when they are helpful to distinguish the recipe, not as an apology.

Acceptable examples:

- Mughlai-Style Chicken Korma
- Aloo Pakora-Inspired Potato Fritters
- Bold Carrot Coconut Peanut Chutney
- Vietnamese Bò Kho-Style Brisket Stew Plate
- Thai-Inspired Bang Bang Shrimp Bowls

Do not add unnecessary public labels just to explain that the recipe is the Amy & Don version. All Flavorweaver recipes already are.

---

## Future Echo Rule

When building or revising Flavorweaver recipes:

- Do not over-explain on public HTML pages.
- Do not add defensive adaptation disclaimers.
- Keep public pages cook-facing and confident.
- Preserve research, adaptation logic, and future-test notes in Markdown.
- Use measurements in ingredient roll-ups, measured ingredients, stage bowls, and cooking steps wherever they prevent kitchen confusion.
- Remember that the archive serves the Amy & Don Kitchen first.

---

## Final Test

A recipe belongs in Flavorweaver when the answer is yes:

> Would Amy and Don be excited to make this again?

If yes, document it well, cook from it confidently, and let it become part of the kitchen.
